lesson_title: Return by reference and return by address

cards.text: Return by reference syntax: {{c1::int& getElement()}}. This returns a {{c2::reference}} to an object, not a copy.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.back: A dangling reference is a reference to an object that has been destroyed. This happens when returning a reference to a local variable, causing undefined behavior.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
int& getLocal() {
int x = 5;
return x; // danger!
}
cards.answer: Returns a reference to a local variable. x is destroyed when function returns, creating a dangling reference.
cards.explanation: Never return a reference to a local variable - it will be destroyed, leaving the reference invalid.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.text: It's safe to return a reference to an object passed by {{c1::reference}} or to a {{c2::static}} or {{c3::global}} variable, but never to a {{c4::local}} variable.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.question: Why is this code safe?
cards.code:
int& max(int& a, int& b) {
return (a > b) ? a : b;
}
cards.answer: Safe because it returns a reference to a parameter, which exists in the caller's scope and won't be destroyed.
cards.explanation: The returned reference points to an object that outlives the function call.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.back: A dangling pointer is a pointer to an object that has been destroyed. Like dangling references, this causes undefined behavior when dereferenced.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.explanation:
Safe to return reference to:
  • Reference parameter (lives in caller scope)
  • Static variable (persists after return)
  • Global variable (persists after return)
  • Dynamically allocated memory (manual management)
NEVER return reference to local variable!
examples: int& max(int& a, int& b) { return (a>b)?a:b; }  // Safe
int& getStatic() { static int x=5; return x; }  // Safe
int& getBad() { int x=5; return x; }            // DANGER!
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.text: A {{c1::dangling}} reference occurs when returning a reference to a {{c2::local variable}} that gets {{c3::destroyed}} when the function returns.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address

cards.text: Safe to return reference to: {{c1::reference parameter}}, {{c2::static}} variable, or {{c3::global}} variable - but never a {{c4::local}} variable.
cards.source: 12.12 - Return by reference and return by address
